ut2-6-1.title: Hands-on: shrink the wavelength to catch small flaws
ut2-6-1.intro: Detection starts with the wave itself: sweep the probe frequency in a transparent slab and watch wavelength stretch and shrink as lambda = v/f. Make lambda short enough to catch a 1 mm reflector in steel, then see how aluminum and water rewrite it at the same frequency.

tasks.label: Raise frequency until lambda in steel drops below 1 mm
tasks.hint: Lambda = v/f. At 5920 m/s in steel you need roughly 6 MHz or more before a 1 mm reflector becomes a strong scatterer.

tasks.label: Pulse steel, aluminum, and water at one frequency
tasks.hint: Hold frequency fixed and switch materials: slower water (about 1480 m/s) packs the crests far closer together than steel.

ut2-6-2.title: Hands-on: resolve near-equal steps on a fine block
ut2-6-2.intro: These steps differ by as little as 2.5 mm, so their backwall echoes arrive only microseconds apart — separating and calling them is resolution plus operator discrimination. Gauge three steps, then commit to which one is thinnest.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three close steps
tasks.hint: Seat the 0-degree probe fully on one step at a time; the backwall echo's time of flight is your thickness reading.

tasks.label: Discriminate and pick the thinnest step
tasks.hint: Compare echo arrival times, not heights — the earliest backwall return marks the thinnest section.

ut2-6-3.title: Hands-on: peak each echo to size by probe motion
ut2-6-3.intro: Echo height alone is not size — what counts is how amplitude changes as the transducer moves. Sweep the 45-degree wedge across three side-drilled holes and mark each echo exactly at its peak, the same probe-motion discipline behind 6 dB-drop sizing.

tasks.label: Walk the beam over hole 1 and mark its echo peak
tasks.hint: Amplitude maximizes when the beam centreline hits the hole; mark within 4 mm of that peak.

tasks.label: Peak hole 2 as amplitude rises, crests, then falls
tasks.hint: Overshoot on purpose once — watching the echo drop past the peak is exactly what the 6 dB-drop method exploits.

tasks.label: Peak and mark hole 3 to finish the sizing scan
tasks.hint: Small probe steps near the crest separate the true peak from its shoulders.

ut2-6-4.title: Hands-on: locate three hidden holes with a 60-degree wedge
ut2-6-4.intro: Turn sweep time into position: each echo's soundpath plus the 60-degree wedge geometry fixes the reflector's depth and surface distance. Search the scan line systematically and mark all three hidden holes where their echoes peak.

tasks.label: Sweep the line and mark flaw 1 at maximum echo
tasks.hint: Move steadily until an echo appears, then jockey the wedge to peak it before pressing Mark.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 2 and read its depth from soundpath
tasks.hint: At 60 degrees, depth = soundpath x cos 60 — exactly half the soundpath on the first leg.

tasks.label: Locate flaw 3 to complete the position map
tasks.hint: Surface distance = soundpath x sin 60; a longer sweep time means a farther, deeper reflector on leg one.
